title: Batch 1 feature follow-ons
description: Umbrella workstream for Feature Batch 1 in PLAN section 46. These features add product surfaces on top of the core spine while reserving several schema hooks from the first migration.
design: Keep each feature self-contained but anchored to core storage, policy, and interface contracts; honor critical M1 schema reservations; do not let showcase surfaces bypass bounded retrieval or explainability.
acceptance_criteria: Child beads cover Features 1-10 with explicit dependencies into the core spine; critical schema-bearing features point back to the schema baseline bead.
notes: Canonical refs: PLAN section 46, especially 46.11 and 46.12.

title: Implement Dream Mode offline synthesis (F1)
description: Implement the idle-time Dream Mode that creates bounded synthetic links between semantically similar memories without foreground agent activity. It should act as a background synthesis pass that enriches the graph without destabilizing truth or latency budgets.
design: Use ANN-guided candidate sampling plus duplicate suppression instead of whole-store scans; keep dream links additive, confidence-weighted, auditable, and subordinate to durable source evidence.
acceptance_criteria: Dream cycles run only under explicit idle or bounded background conditions; dream-created links and merges are inspectable and deduplicated; foreground latency budgets remain intact while the feature is enabled.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.1. Milestone placement: after graph and consolidation foundations exist.

title: Implement offline dream-job scheduler and bounded execution windows
description: Break Dream Mode into the first operational layer: when and how offline synthesis jobs run. This bead should define bounded execution windows, triggers, and cancellation behavior so dream work never leaks onto the foreground path.
design: Keep dream jobs explicitly offline, bounded, and subordinate to the same observability and cancellation rules as other background maintenance work.
acceptance_criteria: Done means dream-job triggers and windows are explicit, tests or scripts show foreground isolation, and logs identify when a dream job ran or was skipped.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement dream synthesis outputs, lineage, and inspect surfaces
description: Define what Dream Mode actually produces once it runs. This bead should describe the offline synthesis outputs, how they cite sources, and how operators inspect what the dream job added.
design: Treat dream outputs as lineage-preserving derived artifacts rather than opaque summaries.
acceptance_criteria: Done means dream outputs and inspect fields are explicit, lineage is preserved, and representative logs or examples show what a dream run produced.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement brain health dashboard (F10)
description: Implement the read-only brain health dashboard that exposes utilization, quality, conflicts, availability posture, and feature maturity in one operator surface. This makes the system inspectable without hiding side effects or forcing operators to re-read code.
design: Keep the dashboard a read-only view over existing health, pressure, and feature-availability primitives; render the same underlying health-report semantics across CLI watch modes and machine-readable surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: The dashboard reports utilization, confidence or strength signals, conflict counts, availability posture, and recent activity from a shared report shape; read-only behavior is preserved; warnings are explicit when degraded visibility limits the view.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.10. Basic support can start earlier, but full availability posture depends on ops surfaces.

title: Implement health metrics, subsystem status, and trend aggregation
description: Break the brain health dashboard into the first data layer: the metrics and trend aggregates it depends on. This bead should define which subsystem health signals are collected and how they roll up into trend views.
design: Reuse observability hooks from caching, ranking, repair, and maintenance work so the dashboard reflects real contract signals instead of vanity metrics.
acceptance_criteria: Done means health metrics and trend aggregates are explicit, sample outputs are named, and tests or scripts prove a representative degraded condition appears in the aggregate view.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement dashboard views, alerts, and operator drill-down paths
description: Turn aggregated health signals into an actionable dashboard surface. This bead should define the main views, alerting conditions, and drill-down paths operators use to inspect a subsystem problem.
design: Treat the dashboard as an operator surface with explicit links to traces, doctor outputs, or repair reports.
acceptance_criteria: Done means views and alerts are explicit, drill-down examples are named, and logging-heavy walkthroughs show how an operator moves from an alert to the underlying evidence.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement cognitive blackboard working-state and checkpoint surfaces
description: Promote Sections 10.5 and 10.6 into a first-class working-state feature family. This bead should define the visible cognitive blackboard, bounded goal-stack checkpoints, pause or resume or abandon semantics, and the distinction between working-state projections and authoritative durable memory truth.
design: Represent a namespace-aware blackboard state per active task or session containing current goal, subgoals, active evidence, active beliefs, unknowns, next action, and blocked reason. Add bounded checkpoint objects at major decisions and tool boundaries, preserve lineage handles rather than copying authoritative truth, and keep pause/resume/abandon behavior explicit and inspectable. Keep named historical snapshots distinct from resumability checkpoints.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead defines blackboard state, checkpoint structure, and lifecycle semantics explicitly; requires resume tests that verify reconstruction without guessing; requires degraded handling for stale or policy-incompatible checkpoints; and requires CLI or MCP inspect examples plus detailed logs for get, pin, dismiss, snapshot, pause, resume, and abandon flows.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 10.5, 10.6, and INDEX.md lines 66-70. This is currently only indirectly represented and needs explicit beads to satisfy full docs-to-beads coverage.

title: Implement belief-history and conflict resolution surfaces (F2)
description: Implement the richer belief-history, conflict-resolution, and version-chain surfaces that sit on top of core contradiction records. It should let users and agents inspect how preferred beliefs evolved without reopening the core contradiction contract.
design: Distinguish duplicate, revision, coexistence, supersession, and authoritative override in user-facing history and resolution flows; keep ranking able to prefer one answer without erasing stored disagreement.
acceptance_criteria: Belief history and resolution surfaces can explain how and why a preferred version emerged; tests cover duplicate-vs-conflict-vs-supersede behavior plus manual and authoritative resolution paths; this feature extends rather than replaces core contradiction storage.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.2 and critical M1 schema additions in 46.12. This bead depends on the core contradiction issue.

title: Implement belief-history storage and version timeline model
description: Break belief versioning into the first durable layer: the history model itself. This bead should describe how belief states and versions are stored over time without collapsing contradiction history.
design: Model belief history as a durable timeline that coexists with contradiction records and preferred-answer selection.
acceptance_criteria: Done means version history storage is explicit, tests prove prior states remain queryable, and logs show a sample belief timeline evolving across updates.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement belief resolution views and historical explain outputs
description: Add user-facing value on top of belief history by exposing resolved views and historical explanations. This bead should show how a user can inspect what was believed when and why.
design: Reuse contradiction and explain contracts so historical belief views remain inspectable and source-backed.
acceptance_criteria: Done means resolved and historical belief views are explicit, examples cover a changing belief over time, and explain outputs show why the active view differs from earlier versions.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement query-by-example recall (F3)
description: Add example-based recall so an existing memory embedding can seed similarity or anti-similarity search. This expands retrieval ergonomics without introducing new storage or bypassing the canonical retrieval contract.
design: Reuse stored embeddings as recall sources for like or unlike queries; keep example-sourced queries inside the same filtering, ranking, explanation, and policy pipeline as text recall.
acceptance_criteria: Like and unlike recall modes work through the same bounded retrieval pipeline as text queries; validation errors prevent ambiguous source combinations; explain output names the example source and search mode clearly.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.3. Milestone placement: retrieval phase after indexed search exists.

title: Implement query-by-example input normalization and seed selection
description: Break query-by-example into the first bounded stage: turning example inputs into retrieval seeds. This bead should define how examples are normalized and which stored evidence becomes the seed set.
design: Keep example ingestion bounded and explicit about what features or fields are used as seeds.
acceptance_criteria: Done means example normalization is explicit, seed selection tests are named, and logs show which stored items became seeds for a representative query.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement query-by-example candidate expansion and explain artifacts
description: Turn the seed set into a bounded retrieval mode with inspectable results. This bead should define how query-by-example expands candidates and how the explanation references the seed evidence.
design: Reuse the ranking and explain contracts so example-based recall stays bounded and debuggable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means candidate expansion caps are explicit, examples show how seed evidence influenced retrieval, and logs capture the example-to-result path.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement context budget API (F4)
description: Implement the context budget API that packs ranked memories into a bounded token budget with explicit reasons for inclusion and omission. This should make the system directly useful to agents without inventing a parallel retrieval contract.
design: Score candidates with relevance, utility, and working-memory overlap penalties while staying inside the canonical retrieval and packaging pipeline; return ready-to-inject output plus machine-readable accounting.
acceptance_criteria: Budget packing respects the declared token budget and exposes what fit, what was omitted, and why; working-memory overlap penalties are visible; the feature does not bypass canonical ranking, policy, or explainability rules.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.4. Milestone placement: MCP-heavy follow-on once retrieval contract and packaging exist.

title: Implement context-budget request contract and allocation semantics
description: Break the context budget feature into the first API-facing contract: how callers ask for budgeted output and what allocation knobs exist. This bead should define budget units, constraints, and what the system is allowed to optimize.
design: Express context budgeting as a structured request contract tied to the retrieval packaging layer rather than a hidden heuristic.
acceptance_criteria: Done means request fields and allocation semantics are explicit, examples cover representative budgets, and tests verify invalid or conflicting budget requests are handled clearly.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement budget-aware result trimming and rationale outputs
description: Turn context budgeting into observable behavior by defining how outputs are trimmed or compressed under a budget. This bead should explain what gets dropped, what gets summarized, and how callers are told about the tradeoff.
design: Reuse explain and packaging contracts so budget-driven trimming stays inspectable instead of feeling random.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the trimming logic is explicit, rationale outputs are named, and logging-heavy examples show how two different budgets change the same result set.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement temporal landmark system (F5)
description: Implement landmark detection and era boundaries so memories can be anchored to meaningful transitions over time. This enriches encoding and recall with temporal structure without making landmarks authoritative truth.
design: Use novelty, arousal, and recent-similarity gates to detect candidate landmarks during encode; store landmark and era metadata as bounded enrichments that support timeline and era-scoped recall.
acceptance_criteria: Landmark and era metadata are present in the schema baseline and tied back to encoded memories; timeline and era-scoped retrieval surfaces can explain why an event became a landmark; landmark metadata remains additive.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.5 and critical M1 schema additions in 46.12.

title: Implement temporal landmark detection and durable landmark storage
description: Break temporal landmarks into the first durable layer: how notable time anchors are detected and stored. This bead should define what counts as a landmark and how it is represented for later recall.
design: Treat landmarks as bounded derived artifacts tied to durable source evidence and explicit scoring rules.
acceptance_criteria: Done means landmark detection rules and storage shape are explicit, tests cover representative landmark creation, and logs show why a sample period became a landmark.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement landmark-assisted temporal recall and explain outputs
description: Turn stored landmarks into user-facing retrieval value. This bead should define how temporal landmarks influence recall and how the result explains which landmarks were used.
design: Reuse retrieval and explain surfaces so landmark-assisted recall stays bounded and inspectable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means temporal recall examples show landmark usage, explain outputs reference the relevant landmarks, and logs demonstrate bounded candidate expansion.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement passive observation mode (F6)
description: Implement a passive observation mode that segments streamed or watched content into memories without explicit remember calls. This lowers adoption friction while preserving provenance, topic boundaries, and policy context.
design: Segment streams with bounded chunking and topic-shift detection rather than full-stream rescans; attach observation source and chunk metadata so derived memories remain traceable to their origin; keep dry-run behavior explicit.
acceptance_criteria: Observed content preserves source metadata, chunk identifiers, and context fields needed for audit or replay; observation mode honors namespace, policy, and bounded encode-path rules; dry-run shows what would be encoded before writes occur.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.6. Milestone placement: daemon or MCP follow-on after core encode semantics exist.

title: Implement passive-observation ingest mode and write gating
description: Break passive observation into the first behavioral change: a mode that observes without behaving like normal active encode. This bead should define what gets recorded, what is suppressed, and which governance gates still apply.
design: Keep passive observation explicit and policy-aware, with clear distinctions between observed signals and actively asserted memories.
acceptance_criteria: Done means passive-observation write behavior is explicit, policy tests cover denied observation cases, and logs show when passive mode captured versus suppressed a signal.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement passive-observation inspect, provenance, and retention semantics
description: Expose the special status of passive observations instead of letting them blend invisibly into normal evidence. This bead should define provenance markers, inspect outputs, and any distinct retention behavior.
design: Represent passive observations with explicit source and policy markers so later recall and forgetting flows can treat them appropriately.
acceptance_criteria: Done means passive-observation artifacts are distinguishable in inspect outputs, retention semantics are named, and scripts show how a passive observation appears end to end.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement memory confidence intervals (F7)
description: Implement the confidence surface that tracks reliability separately from strength. This lets corroboration, reconsolidation churn, and contradiction events shift confidence while keeping the result inspectable and queryable.
design: Model confidence independently from strength; update confidence through corroboration, reconsolidation, and conflict paths using bounded, explainable formulas; expose low-confidence views and filtering without hiding source evidence.
acceptance_criteria: Confidence fields live in the schema baseline and update paths are deterministic and explainable; inspect and stats surfaces show both strength and confidence summaries; retrieval filters can surface uncertainty without silent loss of evidence.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.7 and critical M1 schema additions in 46.12.

title: Implement confidence-interval storage and scoring inputs
description: Break confidence intervals into the first durable and computational layer. This bead should define what interval or uncertainty fields are stored and which evidence contributes to them.
design: Model uncertainty as explicit stored and computed data rather than UI-only decoration.
acceptance_criteria: Done means confidence fields and inputs are explicit, tests cover representative confidence calculations, and logs show how sample evidence affected uncertainty.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement confidence-aware retrieval, explain, and display rules
description: Turn stored uncertainty into retrieval behavior and user-facing explanations. This bead should describe how confidence affects ordering or display and how callers inspect that effect.
design: Reuse ranking and explain surfaces so confidence stays inspectable rather than becoming hidden ranking magic.
acceptance_criteria: Done means confidence-aware examples exist, explain outputs include interval or uncertainty detail, and tests prove uncertainty affects retrieval in the documented way.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement skill extraction from episodic clusters (F8)
description: Implement skill extraction that distills repeated episodic patterns into procedural memories once cluster quality is high enough. This should emerge from consolidation and graph structure, not from unbounded speculative synthesis.
design: Require sufficient cluster size, coherence, and type consistency before drafting a procedural memory; preserve source engram lineage and confidence on any distilled skill artifact.
acceptance_criteria: Extracted procedural memories point back to the source engram and preserve relevant provenance; manual or background extraction flows report extracted and skipped cases; derived skills never become the sole surviving evidence.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.8 and critical M1 schema additions in 46.12. Milestone placement: after graph and consolidation support exist.

title: Implement episodic-cluster analysis for skill candidates
description: Break skill extraction into the first analytic pass: detecting candidate skill patterns from episodic clusters. This bead should define the clustering and extraction heuristics without pretending they are already stable skills.
design: Keep extraction bounded and lineage-preserving so candidate skills remain traceable to their source episodes.
acceptance_criteria: Done means cluster analysis rules are explicit, representative extraction tests are named, and logs show which source episodes produced a sample skill candidate.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement skill artifact storage, review, and recall surfaces
description: Turn candidate skills into explicit artifacts users or operators can inspect and reuse. This bead should describe storage, review, and retrieval semantics for extracted skills.
design: Treat skill artifacts as bounded derived outputs with clear provenance and review hooks.
acceptance_criteria: Done means skill artifacts are explicit, inspect outputs show their lineage, and examples prove a skill can be reviewed and recalled without losing its source context.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement reflection artifacts, procedures, and advisory release rules
description: Promote the missing parts of Section 10.4 Reflection Compiler into an explicit bead. This bead should define how successful and failed episodes are turned into procedures, anti-patterns, and checklists while remaining derived, advisory artifacts rather than raw facts or automatically trusted rules.
design: Extend the skill-extraction path with structured reflection outputs such as Procedure, Checklist, and ReflectionArtifact, each linked back to the goal, action trace, tool outcomes, and quality signal that produced it. Keep release of reflection outputs advisory until repeated usefulness or human approval justifies stronger trust.
acceptance_criteria: Done means reflection artifact classes and release rules are explicit; lineage from source episodes is required; tests or scripts show how a successful and a failed episode both produce inspectable reflection outputs; and explain surfaces make it clear that reflection artifacts are derived advice, not canonical evidence.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 10.4 plus section 46.8. Current skill-extraction beads covered part of the idea but not the explicit advisory reflection-compiler contract.

title: Implement procedural-store schema and pattern-to-action lookup surfaces
description: Implement the procedural-store layer that promotes reviewed skills into an explicit pattern-to-action memory surface with O(1)-style lookup semantics. This bead owns procedural-memory storage, namespace and policy rules for promoted skills, inspectability, and the separation between episodic evidence, extracted candidate skills, and approved procedural entries.
design: Keep procedural memory distinct from episodic or semantic recall. Store approved procedures in a dedicated schema or lookup surface keyed by stable pattern handles, preserve lineage back to the extracted-skill evidence and review decision, keep policy and namespace scope explicit, and avoid routing ordinary recall through this store unless the caller explicitly asks for procedural lookup or the core contract says that shortcut is safe.
acceptance_criteria: Done means reviewed skills can be promoted into a dedicated procedural store with stable lookup keys, retrieval tests prove direct pattern-to-action lookup does not require full recall traversal, inspect and audit surfaces preserve lineage to source episodes and review approvals, and logging-heavy end-to-end checks show promotion, lookup, denial, and rollback of procedural entries.
notes: Why: PLAN sections 2.2, 5.1, and 5.2 explicitly include a procedural store, but the current skill-extraction beads stopped at extracted artifacts and advisory release rules. This bead closes the gap from skill candidate to actual procedural-memory surface. Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 2.2, 5.1, and 5.2.

title: Implement cross-agent memory sharing (F9)
description: Implement cross-agent sharing with namespace and visibility semantics so multiple agents can safely reuse shared memories. This turns the system into a governed collective memory layer rather than a single-agent store.
design: Use namespace, agent, and visibility fields reserved in the schema baseline instead of ad hoc sharing flags; keep share and unshare behavior inside the canonical policy and explainability contract.
acceptance_criteria: Shared, private, and public visibility semantics are deterministic and parity-tested across interfaces; namespace enforcement happens before expensive retrieval and is auditable on denial paths; share or unshare operations emit governance artifacts.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 46.9 and critical M1 schema additions in 46.12. This feature is governance-heavy and cites the policy matrix bead.

title: Implement namespace-aware sharing model and access mediation
description: Break cross-agent sharing into the first policy-critical layer: the sharing model itself. This bead should define how shared memories are exposed across agents or namespaces and what mediation occurs before access is granted.
design: Use the canonical governance model so sharing stays subordinate to namespace, visibility, and retention policy instead of inventing a new side channel.
acceptance_criteria: Done means sharing semantics are explicit, denial cases are named, and dedicated isolation tests prove unauthorized access is blocked early.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement share, deny, redact, and audit surfaces across interfaces
description: Turn the sharing model into real interface behavior. This bead should define how share requests succeed, fail, redact, and get audited across CLI and MCP surfaces.
design: Keep share outcomes inspectable and parity-tested so interfaces do not drift in how they present shared memory behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Done means share and denial outputs are explicit, audit records are required, and logging-heavy examples prove parity across at least two public surfaces.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Bounded encode and retrieval engine
description: Umbrella workstream for the measurable encode, storage, retrieval, ranking, interface, contradiction, graph, and cache spine. It turns the canonical contract into a bounded engine with explainable outputs.
design: Follow PLAN section 44 execution order; make every request-path step measurable, inspectable, and policy-aware before optimizing; keep interfaces thin over shared core semantics.
acceptance_criteria: Child beads cover Tier1, Tier2, retrieval packaging, ranking, contradiction, interfaces, graph, and cache work; dependencies preserve the core spine before optional feature work.
notes: Canonical refs: PLAN sections 15-20, 33-39, 44; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md restriction and interface rows.

title: Implement Tier1 fast encode plus exact/recent recall
description: Build the bounded Tier1 path for fast encode and exact or recent recall so the system has a measurable hot-path MVP. This establishes the minimal low-latency surface required by the phase gates before richer indexed retrieval lands.
design: Follow the canonical encode and Tier1 access patterns from the plan; keep Tier1 metadata-focused, policy-aware, and free of giant payload ownership; expose exact and recent retrieval behavior for later reuse.
acceptance_criteria: Encode and Tier1 recall operate within declared hot-path budgets and candidate bounds; exact and recent retrieval honor namespace and policy context before expensive follow-on work; metrics show the Tier1 path and latency.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 17, 36.1, 36.3, 38, 40.1, 44 step 3.

title: Implement normalize, fingerprint, classify, and provisional-salience fast path
description: Split the Tier1 encode path into the minimal bounded steps that must happen synchronously before persistence. This bead should describe how raw events become normalized, fingerprinted, shallow-classified, and assigned a provisional salience signal without dragging in heavyweight enrichment.
design: Keep the implementation narrow, synchronous, and measurable so later enrichment work can remain deferred while the initial encode path stays fast.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the exact synchronous steps, requires unit tests for normalization and fingerprint stability, and requires structured logs or traces that show this fast path stayed within its candidate and latency budget.
notes: Refs: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/PSEUDOCODE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 17, 36, and 38. The Tier1 path must stay bounded, measurable, and free of heavyweight payload work.

title: Implement Tier1 hot metadata store and session windows
description: Create the Tier1 hot set structure that holds recent metadata and session-local lookup state without owning heavyweight payloads. This bead should explain what belongs in Tier1, what must stay out, and how session locality stays fast and bounded.
design: Model Tier1 as a hot metadata and handle layer that supports exact, recent, and small session lookups while leaving large durable truth to deeper tiers.
acceptance_criteria: Done means Tier1 storage boundaries are explicit, request-path tests prove that large payloads are not fetched prematurely, and logs show session-window behavior and hot-set hit or miss evidence.
notes: Refs: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/PSEUDOCODE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 17, 36, and 38. The Tier1 path must stay bounded, measurable, and free of heavyweight payload work.

title: Implement exact-id, recent, and small-lookup Tier1 planner
description: Turn the Tier1 use cases into a deterministic planner instead of scattered if-statements. This bead should cover exact lookup, recent recall, and small active-session reads that should terminate before more expensive Tier2 or graph work begins.
design: Describe the branching rules, cutoffs, and fallback conditions that decide when Tier1 alone should answer versus when deeper retrieval must start.
acceptance_criteria: Done means planner branches are explicit, unit tests cover the small-lookup cases, and explain traces show when Tier1 answered directly versus when it routed to deeper work.
notes: Refs: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/PSEUDOCODE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 17, 36, and 38. The Tier1 path must stay bounded, measurable, and free of heavyweight payload work.

title: Implement Tier1 latency, candidate-budget, and route tracing
description: Attach observability to the Tier1 path so regressions are obvious. This bead should name the metrics and traces that prove Tier1 stayed within latency budgets, preserved candidate counts, and routed correctly.
design: Instrument the encode and Tier1 recall path with per-request traces and counters that later benchmark and failure beads can reuse.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead requires integration tests or scripts that emit detailed logs for Tier1 route choice, latency, and candidate counts, and those signals are named clearly enough to detect regressions after landing.
notes: Refs: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/PSEUDOCODE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 17, 36, and 38. The Tier1 path must stay bounded, measurable, and free of heavyweight payload work.

title: Implement working-memory admission buffer and overflow-to-encode triggers
description: Implement the bounded working-memory controller that sits between raw intake and durable encode. This bead owns the small attended-item buffer, admission thresholds, overflow behavior, and explicit promotion-or-discard rules so the encode path starts from a visible controller state instead of pretending every input becomes durable memory.
design: Keep working memory ephemeral and bounded. Model a small slot-limited controller (brain-inspired 7±2, implemented as a practical configurable cap), explicit attention/admission gating, pinned-versus-evictable items, overflow-to-encode triggers, and discard semantics for items that never clear the threshold. Do not let this layer silently persist items or become a second hidden store.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core code exposes working-memory types and admission APIs, deterministic tests cover below-threshold discard, overflow, explicit promotion to encode, and no-durable-write-before-admission behavior, and logging-heavy integration checks show why an item was dropped, buffered, or promoted. Acceptance also requires machine-readable traces for slot pressure, threshold decisions, and overflow routing.
notes: Why: PLAN sections 2.2, 4.4, and 5.2 give working memory a first-class role, but the backlog previously jumped straight from raw input to encode fast-path logic. This bead restores that missing controller layer so later blackboard and retrieval work do not improvise their own admission semantics. Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 2.2, 4.4, 5.2, and docs/ARCHITECTURE.md write-path contract.

title: Implement fastembed integration and generation-aware embedding cache
description: Implement the local fastembed integration and generation-aware embedding cache used by encode, recall, and background derivation flows. This bead owns content-versus-context embedding purpose separation, cache-key generation binding, batch behavior, and the rule that the canonical request path never falls back to a remote embedder.
design: Provide one core embedder abstraction backed by fastembed, with cache keys bound to normalized input bytes, embedder generation, vector dimension, and embedding purpose. Support local cache hits for content and context embeddings, batch embedding for consolidation/import, and authoritative float32 embeddings plus derived quantized search projections. Keep remote APIs completely out of the canonical encode fast path.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core code exposes a reusable local embedder interface, generation-aware cache keys and invalidation hooks exist, deterministic tests cover cache hit versus miss, model-generation change, purpose separation, and batch embedding behavior, and structured logs show local-only embedding, cache outcomes, and no remote fallback on encode fast paths.
notes: Why: PLAN sections 4.4, 5.2, and 7.5 make fastembed and generation-aware embedding caches part of the core stack, but those details were not explicit in code-oriented beads. This bead gives later semantic retrieval, cache, and reconsolidation work a real implementation substrate instead of prose-only assumptions. Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 4.4, 5.2, 7.5.

title: Implement Tier2 durable storage, tiering, and indexed retrieval
description: Build the durable indexed store and tiering behavior that makes retrieval scale beyond the Tier1 hot set. This establishes Tier2 truth, filtering, and indexable search without sacrificing rebuildability or bounded work.
design: Separate metadata and filtering state from larger payloads so request-path prefilters stay bounded; implement traceable promotion and demotion decisions; keep indexes rebuildable from durable truth.
acceptance_criteria: Tier2 durable storage and indexed retrieval support the phase-1 gate requirements for indexed, session, and entity queries; tier routing decisions are inspectable and rebuild or migration notes preserve durable truth.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 17, 18, 33, 38, 40.2, 44 step 4; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md storage and schema rows.

title: Implement durable memory item layout and payload separation
description: Break the Tier2 durable model into the first concrete storage task: how metadata, filters, and heavyweight payloads are physically separated. This bead should make hot-path-safe prefiltering possible without full payload reads.
design: Define the durable item layout, payload indirection, and storage boundaries so later index and retrieval planners can operate on compact metadata first and only fetch payloads after the final cut.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the durable layout, requires schema or storage tests showing payload separation, and requires logs or traces that prove the request path is not decompressing or loading heavyweight content too early.
notes: Refs: docs/STORAGE.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, docs/DATA_SCHEMAS.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 17, 18, 33, and 38. Preserve rebuildability and keep prefilters hot-path-safe.

title: Implement Tier2 exact, temporal, entity, and namespace prefilter indexes
description: Materialize the first bounded index families that keep Tier2 retrieval off the full-scan path. This bead should explain which index families exist, what they prefilter on, and why those families are enough for the initial planner set.
design: Build explicit prefilter indexes for exact keys, time windows, entities, and namespace or policy-aware cuts while keeping them rebuildable from durable truth.
acceptance_criteria: Done means each index family has declared coverage, rebuild expectations, and benchmark or integration checks showing candidate generation stays bounded without full-store scans.
notes: Refs: docs/STORAGE.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, docs/DATA_SCHEMAS.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 17, 18, 33, and 38. Preserve rebuildability and keep prefilters hot-path-safe.

title: Implement promotion, demotion, and lifecycle-aware tier routing
description: Define how items move through tiers without hiding the routing decision. This bead should explain how salience, recency, lifecycle state, and payload size influence placement and movement between hot and durable layers.
design: Keep tier routing traceable and bounded, with explicit promotion and demotion outcomes rather than implicit side effects spread across retrieval code.
acceptance_criteria: Done means routing rules are explicit, lifecycle-aware tests are named, and logs show why a sample item was promoted, demoted, or kept in place.
notes: Refs: docs/STORAGE.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, docs/DATA_SCHEMAS.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 17, 18, 33, and 38. Preserve rebuildability and keep prefilters hot-path-safe.

title: Implement bounded Tier2 candidate generation and query planners
description: Turn the Tier2 retrieval modes into explicit bounded planners that select the right prefilters and candidate caps. This bead should describe how exact, temporal, entity-heavy, hybrid, and fallback retrieval paths compose without doing unbounded work.
design: Implement planner branches and candidate generation steps that start with the narrowest prefilters, preserve candidate counts, and stop before any full scan or uncapped graph expansion can happen.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the planner branches are inspectable, each mode has declared caps, and end-to-end scripts emit detailed logs showing prefilter choice, candidate counts, and fallback behavior.
notes: Refs: docs/STORAGE.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, docs/DATA_SCHEMAS.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 17, 18, 33, and 38. Preserve rebuildability and keep prefilters hot-path-safe.

title: Define Tier2 rebuild, migration, and backfill contract
description: Freeze the maintenance contract for Tier2 before deeper features accumulate on top of it. This bead should say how Tier2 indexes and derived helpers are rebuilt, how migrations backfill safely, and what rollback evidence is required.
design: Treat rebuildability as part of the durable storage design by naming the source of truth, rebuild entrypoints, expected artifacts, and migration rollback boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: Done means later repair and migration beads can point to a single Tier2 rebuild contract, and the bead explicitly requires deterministic rebuild or migration validation plus operator-visible logging.
notes: Refs: docs/STORAGE.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, docs/DATA_SCHEMAS.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 17, 18, 33, and 38. Preserve rebuildability and keep prefilters hot-path-safe.

title: Define retrieval result, explainability, and packaging contract
description: Freeze the canonical recall request and result shapes, including explainability, omission summaries, policy summaries, and packaging rules. This gives CLI, MCP, and JSON-RPC one shared retrieval surface instead of divergent wrappers.
design: Define retrieval planner and packaging families around bounded candidate pipelines and explainable output; carry policy filtering, provenance, contradiction markers, freshness markers, and deferred payload behavior in the result envelope.
acceptance_criteria: The canonical recall request and retrieval result shapes are explicit enough for cross-surface parity tests; output semantics cover omissions, denials, contradictions, freshness, lineage, and packaging decisions.

title: Define retrieval result envelope and provenance payload contract
description: Describe the structured retrieval payload that every public surface should return or adapt. This bead should cover result identifiers, provenance fragments, lineage hooks, contradiction state, and the core metadata later explain surfaces depend on.
design: Standardize the retrieval envelope so callers receive enough structured evidence to understand what was returned before ranking and packaging details diverge across surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead defines the stable result envelope, requires schema or serialization tests, and names sample outputs that future CLI and MCP surfaces must preserve.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/MCP_API.md, docs/CLI.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 34, and 35. Retrieval outputs must carry enough structured evidence that CLI and MCP surfaces never become black boxes.

title: Define explain trace schema for scores, policies, and route decisions
description: Make explainability explicit in the payload contract rather than something hand-built by each interface. This bead should define how route decisions, score components, policy filters, and fallback reasons are represented.
design: Treat explain traces as structured data with declared fields and nesting so they remain stable across CLI, MCP, and future inspect surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the explain schema is explicit, includes policy and route detail, and has tests or golden examples showing how traces should look for representative retrieval paths.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/MCP_API.md, docs/CLI.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 34, and 35. Retrieval outputs must carry enough structured evidence that CLI and MCP surfaces never become black boxes.

title: Define redaction, packaging, and cross-surface serialization rules
description: Freeze how retrieval results are packaged for each surface without losing policy meaning. This bead should explain when fields are redacted, how explain traces are trimmed or preserved, and how structured results serialize across CLI and MCP boundaries.
design: Describe redaction and packaging as policy-aware serialization rules rather than ad hoc output formatting so parity remains testable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names serialization rules for at least CLI and MCP surfaces, requires denial or redaction examples, and requires parity tests with detailed logging of packaged outputs.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/MCP_API.md, docs/CLI.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 15, 34, and 35. Retrieval outputs must carry enough structured evidence that CLI and MCP surfaces never become black boxes.

title: Implement dual memory output evidence-pack and action-pack contract
description: Promote Section 10.1 Dual Memory Output into a first-class retrieval packaging bead. This bead should define the parallel evidence pack and action pack structure so the system clearly separates what it knows from what it suggests doing, instead of burying suggested actions inside evidence-heavy result blobs.
design: Extend the retrieval envelope with explicit EvidenceItem and ActionArtifact groupings, mode flags such as strict, balanced, and fast, and supporting-evidence links from every action artifact back to the evidence set that justified it. Keep action artifacts derived, inspectable, and never authoritative over the underlying evidence.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead defines evidence_pack, action_pack, and uncertainty fields explicitly; names the mode semantics; requires serialization and explain examples; and requires logging-heavy end-to-end scripts showing how action artifacts are linked back to supporting evidence and how stale or weak support lowers confidence or suppresses action suggestions.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 10.1 plus sections 15, 33, 34, and 35. This closes the remaining gap where retrieval packaging existed but Dual Memory Output was not a first-class bead.

title: Implement canonical RetrievalResult structs and serialization codecs
description: Implement the canonical RetrievalResult code types instead of leaving them as doc-only envelope prose. This bead owns the shared structs and codecs for outcome_class, bounded evidence_pack, optional action_pack, omission and deferred-payload state, policy and provenance summaries, freshness markers, conflict markers, metrics, and explain handles or embedded explanation families.
design: Make the retrieval envelope a transport-neutral core contract with serde-compatible codecs and explicit versionable field families. Wrappers may render or summarize it differently, but they must serialize the same semantic object. Keep the types narrow enough that ranking, inspect, explain, and interface beads can all depend on them without creating parallel DTO trees.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core exports canonical RetrievalResult-related structs, round-trip serialization tests prove the shared envelope is stable, and later CLI or MCP work can reuse the same types directly. Tests must cover full, partial, miss, degraded, and policy-filtered outcomes plus presence and absence of action_pack, omitted_summary, and explain_handle families.
notes: Why: retrieval packaging is central to the product contract, yet the backlog previously froze only the prose contract. This bead adds the real code types needed before interface parity and explain surfaces can be implemented cleanly. Refs: docs/MCP_API.md common response envelope, docs/CLI.md retrieval contract, docs/TEST_STRATEGY.md recall quality and parity matrices.

title: Implement explain-trace, omission-summary, and policy-summary builders
description: Implement the builder layer that assembles machine-readable explain-trace, omitted-result, and policy-summary families from core retrieval state. This bead turns the documented explanation field families into reusable code so inspect, explain, CLI JSON, and MCP responses can expose one shared structure.
design: Build focused helpers that translate ranking, routing, policy, cache, and degraded-mode state into the canonical summary families named in the docs. Keep these helpers in core observability and packaging code rather than in interface adapters, and make them cheap enough to support summary versus full explanation modes without changing retrieval semantics.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core can assemble route_summary, result_reasons, omitted_summary, policy_summary, provenance_summary, freshness_markers, conflict_markers, and trace_stages from shared state, and focused tests prove stable field names plus bounded summary versus full modes. Include coverage for denied or redacted candidates, cache bypass and stale-warning paths, degraded serving, and contradiction-bearing result sets.
notes: Why: the docs already specify rich explanation families, but there was no concrete implementation bead for the code that builds them. This bead keeps explanation logic centralized and inspectable instead of letting each interface invent bespoke payloads. Refs: docs/MCP_API.md explanation families, docs/CLI.md explain modes, docs/TEST_STRATEGY.md observability and recall-quality matrices.

title: Implement ranking and inspect/explain surfaces
description: Build the measurable ranking baseline and the inspect or explain surfaces that show why a result was returned. This keeps scoring simple, bounded, and auditable before richer rerank behavior is added.
design: Implement canonical ranking inputs and bounded rerank strategy from the plan; expose baseline score components, rerank deltas, and final ordering reasons; keep contradiction, provenance, graph support, and policy filters visible.
acceptance_criteria: Ranking is measurable and explainable enough for the phase-1 and phase-2 gates; formula tests and latency benchmarks exist; no result ordering relies on an uninspectable black box.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 16, 36.5, 37, 40.2-40.3, 44 step 5; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md scoring and observability requirements.

title: Implement base recency, salience, strength, and provenance formulas
description: Break the ranking baseline into explicit score components that can be tested independently. This bead should define how recency, salience, strength, and provenance contribute before any richer reranking logic is layered on top.
design: Translate the canonical formulas into deterministic scoring functions with documented inputs, output ranges, and explain-field names.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the formulas, requires unit tests for score calculations, and emits score breakdown logs or fixtures that later inspect surfaces can reuse.
notes: Refs: docs/RANKING_FORMULAS.md, docs/ALGORITHM_CATALOG.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 16, 36, and 37. Every scoring rule needs explicit formulas, tests, and explain artifacts.

title: Implement bounded rerank blend and candidate-cut rules
description: Add the bounded rerank layer that combines candidate evidence without turning ranking into an uninspectable black box. This bead should explain how much reranking is allowed, what the candidate cut is, and which inputs are legal on the request path.
design: Keep reranking lightweight and bounded by explicit candidate limits while preserving component-wise explainability.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the rerank blend and cut rules are explicit, performance tests are named, and detailed logs show pre-cut candidates, post-cut candidates, and the score deltas that changed ordering.
notes: Refs: docs/RANKING_FORMULAS.md, docs/ALGORITHM_CATALOG.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 16, 36, and 37. Every scoring rule needs explicit formulas, tests, and explain artifacts.

title: Implement inspect and explain surfaces for ranking decisions
description: Expose ranking behavior through stable inspect or explain outputs instead of forcing operators to read code. This bead should show how final ordering, component scores, rerank deltas, and policy filters are surfaced to callers.
design: Build dedicated inspect surfaces that reuse the structured explain schema and highlight what changed a result order without inventing a new hidden ranking vocabulary.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead requires integration tests or scripts that print detailed ranking explanations, and those outputs are rich enough to debug ordering disputes without attaching a debugger.
notes: Refs: docs/RANKING_FORMULAS.md, docs/ALGORITHM_CATALOG.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 16, 36, and 37. Every scoring rule needs explicit formulas, tests, and explain artifacts.

title: Implement ranking calibration fixtures and score-breakdown artifacts
description: Turn ranking quality work into reproducible fixtures rather than intuition. This bead should capture calibration datasets, expected score relations, and artifact formats that future tuning work can compare without reopening the formula contract.
design: Define small but representative ranking fixtures and artifact shapes that show component scores, final order, and any benchmark metadata needed to rerun the comparison.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the fixtures and artifact format, and later benchmark or quality beads can consume them directly for regression detection.
notes: Refs: docs/RANKING_FORMULAS.md, docs/ALGORITHM_CATALOG.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 16, 36, and 37. Every scoring rule needs explicit formulas, tests, and explain artifacts.

title: Implement contradiction records and conflict-aware storage
description: Implement the core contradiction representation that keeps disagreement durable, inspectable, and policy-aware. This bead satisfies the canonical rule that the system must never silently overwrite conflicting information.
design: Store contradiction and preferred-answer metadata durably enough for retrieval, ranking, and governance; keep conflict-aware storage separate from user-facing belief-history convenience surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Conflicting writes produce durable contradiction records instead of in-place overwrite; retrieval and ranking can surface preferred answers without deleting disagreement; phase-2 gate evidence shows contradiction records exist.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 12, 15, 24, 40.3, 44 step 6; this is the core prerequisite beneath richer belief-versioning surfaces.

title: Implement contradiction record schema and write-path branching
description: Make contradiction handling concrete at write time instead of as a later clean-up problem. This bead should explain how conflicting writes branch into durable contradiction records without destroying prior evidence.
design: Define contradiction rows or linked records, write-path branching behavior, and the minimal metadata needed for later preferred-answer, audit, and retention work.
acceptance_criteria: Done means conflicting writes create explicit contradiction artifacts, unit or integration tests prove no silent overwrite occurs, and logs show the branch taken when a contradiction is detected.
notes: Refs: docs/MEMORY_MODEL.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 12, 13, 24, and 40. Contradictions must remain durable, inspectable, and policy-aware instead of being silently overwritten.

title: Implement preferred-answer, confidence, and active-contradiction selection
description: Layer a user-facing selection model on top of the contradiction records without erasing disagreement. This bead should describe how a preferred answer, confidence signal, or active contradiction marker is chosen while keeping the underlying evidence intact.
design: Separate the convenience surface from the durable contradiction store so retrieval can show a preferred answer without deleting alternate claims.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the selection rules are explicit, tests prove disagreement remains queryable, and explain outputs show why one answer was preferred at a given moment.
notes: Refs: docs/MEMORY_MODEL.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 12, 13, 24, and 40. Contradictions must remain durable, inspectable, and policy-aware instead of being silently overwritten.

title: Implement contradiction explain, audit, and lineage surfaces
description: Expose contradiction state as a first-class inspect surface rather than something hidden in raw rows. This bead should show how operators or users can inspect disagreement, lineage, and resolution history.
design: Reuse the structured explain and provenance contracts so contradiction views include source evidence, branch history, and any preferred-answer rationale.
acceptance_criteria: Done means contradiction inspection outputs are named, golden examples or scripts are required, and audit records prove disagreement history remains durable and readable.
notes: Refs: docs/MEMORY_MODEL.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 12, 13, 24, and 40. Contradictions must remain durable, inspectable, and policy-aware instead of being silently overwritten.

title: Implement contradiction retention, archive, and policy behavior
description: Attach governance and lifecycle semantics to contradiction data before forgetting or archive work builds on it. This bead should describe what happens to contradictions under retention changes, archives, restores, or legal holds.
design: Treat contradiction records as policy-sensitive durable evidence that must obey governance rules without silently disappearing.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names archive and retention behavior for contradictions, requires policy-focused tests, and explicitly prevents silent destruction of the last authoritative evidence in a conflict set.
notes: Refs: docs/MEMORY_MODEL.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 12, 13, 24, and 40. Contradictions must remain durable, inspectable, and policy-aware instead of being silently overwritten.

title: Implement CLI contract parity surfaces
description: Implement the CLI surface as a faithful wrapper around the shared core and retrieval contracts. It owns flag, output, validation, and error semantics on the command-line path.
design: Follow the detailed CLI contract and keep commands aligned with shared core behavior; separate validation failures, policy denials, and internal errors; keep destructive flows explicit and inspectable.
acceptance_criteria: CLI commands, flags, and outputs match the shared contract for encode, recall, inspect, explain, and management flows; error classes distinguish validation, policy, and internal failure; parity tests show no silent divergence.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 35 and 42; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md interface-contract row and code-quality gates.

title: Implement canonical CLI commands for encode, recall, inspect, and explain
description: Break the CLI contract into the first user-facing command surface needed for the core system. This bead should define the canonical encode, recall, inspect, and explain commands and the structured outputs they owe.
design: Map the core retrieval and explain contracts onto explicit CLI commands and flags without inventing a separate semantic model for the command line.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the primary commands, their envelopes, and sample outputs, and requires end-to-end scripts that log command invocation, response shape, and denial behavior.
notes: Refs: docs/CLI.md, docs/CONTRIBUTING.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 35 and 42. CLI work packets must include logging-heavy end-to-end scripts and parity checks with the canonical contract.

title: Implement CLI admin surfaces for maintenance, benchmark, and doctor flows
description: Extend the CLI beyond core recall so operators can drive maintenance and validation work without hidden entrypoints. This bead should cover the maintenance, benchmark, repair, and doctor-oriented commands that later ops beads will rely on.
design: Use the public API and error taxonomy contract to keep these commands consistent with the rest of the CLI surface.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the admin commands, expected outputs, and failure categories, and requires scripts with detailed logging for successful and denied operator flows.
notes: Refs: docs/CLI.md, docs/CONTRIBUTING.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 35 and 42. CLI work packets must include logging-heavy end-to-end scripts and parity checks with the canonical contract.

title: Implement CLI parity, denial, and logging-heavy end-to-end scripts
description: Attach explicit validation to the CLI surface so parity and policy behavior do not drift silently. This bead should define the logging-heavy end-to-end scripts that demonstrate CLI parity with the canonical retrieval and governance contracts.
design: Treat CLI validation as a first-class deliverable by naming the test scripts, expected logs, and parity comparisons they must produce.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead lists concrete end-to-end script scenarios, including denial and redaction cases, and later implementation work can close only after those scripts pass with detailed logs.
notes: Refs: docs/CLI.md, docs/CONTRIBUTING.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 35 and 42. CLI work packets must include logging-heavy end-to-end scripts and parity checks with the canonical contract.

title: Implement MCP and JSON-RPC contract parity surfaces
description: Implement the MCP and JSON-RPC surfaces as parity-preserving wrappers over shared core semantics. It owns envelopes, machine-readable results, policy context propagation, and cross-surface equivalence checks.
design: Follow the detailed API contract so request context, namespace, policy, and explainability survive transport boundaries; keep tool or RPC envelopes stable and explicit about blocked, degraded, or rejected outcomes.
acceptance_criteria: MCP and JSON-RPC requests preserve namespace and policy context and return shared retrieval or mutation semantics; machine-readable envelopes are stable; parity validation covers errors, denials, and explain output.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 34, 42, and 44 step 12; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md interface and namespace rows.

title: Implement MCP/JSON-RPC method envelopes and request schemas
description: Break the external programmatic contract into explicit method envelopes and request shapes. This bead should cover the baseline MCP or JSON-RPC methods for encode, recall, inspect, explain, and policy-aware mutation flows.
design: Use the shared retrieval and packaging contracts so the programmatic API inherits the same semantics and error categories as the CLI surface.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the canonical methods and request or response schema expectations, and requires parity fixtures that later server work can reuse.
notes: Refs: docs/MCP_API.md and docs/PLAN.md sections 34 and 42. Method envelopes, resources, denial paths, and error categories must match the canonical parity rules.

title: Implement MCP resources, streaming surfaces, and inspect payloads
description: Extend the MCP contract with resources and richer inspect payloads rather than limiting it to simple request-response calls. This bead should describe any resource surfaces, streaming traces, and inspect payload variants the MCP layer needs.
design: Keep streaming or resource variants subordinate to the same structured explain and packaging rules so parity remains testable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means resource names and payload shapes are explicit, inspect outputs reuse the canonical explain fields, and representative examples are required for later integration tests.
notes: Refs: docs/MCP_API.md and docs/PLAN.md sections 34 and 42. Method envelopes, resources, denial paths, and error categories must match the canonical parity rules.

title: Implement MCP/daemon parity, denial, and error-model tests
description: Make parity and failure behavior explicit before the MCP layer sprawls. This bead should name the tests that prove standalone, daemon, and MCP surfaces agree on denials, redactions, and error categorization.
design: Use the shared error taxonomy and deterministic-test contracts to define cross-surface parity cases and expected logs.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead defines parity scenarios, denial outputs, and error-class assertions with detailed logs, and later MCP work cannot close without running them.
notes: Refs: docs/MCP_API.md and docs/PLAN.md sections 34 and 42. Method envelopes, resources, denial paths, and error categories must match the canonical parity rules.

title: Implement preflight.run, preflight.explain, and preflight.allow interface parity
description: Turn the preflight safeguard contract into a concrete cross-surface interface bead. This bead should define the preflight APIs and parity rules for CLI, MCP, and JSON-RPC so risky actions and high-stakes action guidance cannot skip shared blocked-action checks by choosing a different wrapper.
design: Implement preflight.run, preflight.explain, and preflight.allow or equivalent surfaces using the shared safeguard vocabulary and exact-scope confirmation semantics from the contract bead. Ensure that preview-only, blocked, degraded, and force-confirmed states serialize consistently across transports.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names request and response shapes for the preflight APIs; requires parity tests with detailed logs across at least CLI and MCP or JSON-RPC; and proves that force-confirm cannot override policy, namespace, retention, or legal-hold constraints.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 10.7 plus sections 34 and 35. This keeps the preflight design from staying purely conceptual.

title: Implement Tokio daemon runtime, Unix-socket lifecycle, and task supervision
description: Implement the Tokio daemon runtime, Unix-socket lifecycle, and background-task supervision layer that the CLI, daemon, and MCP surfaces all rely on. This bead owns process lifecycle, concurrent request handling, graceful shutdown, bounded worker queues, and safe coexistence of request-path reads with background maintenance jobs.
design: Keep transport semantics thin while giving the runtime one concrete async substrate. Use Tokio for socket accept loops, request isolation, task supervision, cancellation propagation, WAL-friendly concurrency, and degraded or read-only posture transitions. The runtime may host CLI-invoked daemon mode, JSON-RPC, and MCP adapters, but it must not invent different product semantics from membrain-core.
acceptance_criteria: Done means a Tokio-based daemon runtime boots and serves requests over the planned local transport, background maintenance jobs can run without blocking bounded reads, graceful shutdown and restart preserve authoritative durability guarantees, and integration tests cover concurrent readers plus background tasks, Unix-socket lifecycle, degraded-mode transitions, and structured runtime logs for queue depth, task cancellation, and request correlation.
notes: Why: PLAN sections 5.3 and 7.1 explicitly choose Rust + Tokio for always-on daemon and background work, but the bead graph mostly covered envelopes and parity tests rather than the async runtime itself. This bead makes the runtime a first-class implementation concern instead of an implied detail. Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 5.3 and 7.1.

title: Implement bounded association graph recall
description: Implement graph-assisted retrieval as a bounded, optional extension of the indexed retrieval path. It enables associative recall and richer explanation without reintroducing unbounded graph walks.
design: Use the graph only behind hard depth, node, and fanout limits seeded from an already-authorized shortlist; keep graph edges derivable from durable evidence and repairable when they drift.
acceptance_criteria: Graph expansion stays within explicit node, depth, and latency budgets; the graph can be rebuilt or repaired from durable truth; isolation tests prove graph edges cannot leak across namespaces or policy boundaries.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 19, 23, 40.3, 44 step 7; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md no-unbounded-graph-walk rule.

title: Implement association edge schema and formation rules
description: Break graph support into its first durable piece: how associations are represented and formed. This bead should describe what edge types exist, what evidence forms them, and how they remain rebuildable instead of magical.
design: Model graph edges as derived but durable-enough structures with clear source lineage and bounded formation rules.
acceptance_criteria: Done means edge types and formation inputs are explicit, derived-state tests are named, and the bead states how edges can be rebuilt from durable truth if the graph is lost.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 19 and 38. Graph support is valuable only while depth, node, and latency budgets stay explicit and measurable.

title: Implement bounded graph expansion planner with depth and node caps
description: Turn graph usage into a bounded planner instead of a free-form traversal. This bead should describe how graph expansion starts, when it stops, and how depth and node caps are enforced on every path.
design: Keep graph expansion subordinate to hard depth and node budgets, with explicit cutoff reasons and fallback behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Done means graph traversal caps are explicit, integration tests prove the caps hold, and logs show depth, node counts, and cutoff reasons for representative requests.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 19 and 38. Graph support is valuable only while depth, node, and latency budgets stay explicit and measurable.

title: Implement graph-backed explain surfaces and cutoff reporting
description: Expose graph participation to users and operators instead of hiding it inside ranking internals. This bead should show which edges were followed, where traversal stopped, and how graph evidence influenced the result.
design: Reuse the explain schema so graph-specific details appear as structured fields rather than ad hoc debug strings.
acceptance_criteria: Done means graph explain outputs are explicit, cutoff reporting is mandatory, and scripts or examples show how graph-assisted recall differs from non-graph recall.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 19 and 38. Graph support is valuable only while depth, node, and latency budgets stay explicit and measurable.

title: Implement graph rebuild hooks and failure-injection tests
description: Connect graph support to the future repair story before it becomes operational debt. This bead should define how graph structures are rebuilt and how corruption or partial rebuild failures are tested.
design: Treat the graph as derived state with explicit rebuild entrypoints and failure scenarios that operators can diagnose.
acceptance_criteria: Done means rebuild hooks are named, failure-injection tests are required, and the bead says which logs or metrics confirm a graph repair run succeeded or failed safely.
notes: Refs: docs/RETRIEVAL.md, docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 19 and 38. Graph support is valuable only while depth, node, and latency budgets stay explicit and measurable.

title: Implement cache, prefetch, and cache observability
description: Implement the bounded cache and prefetch families that accelerate retrieval without changing correctness. It keeps cache behavior version-aware, namespace-aware, repair-aware, and fully observable.
design: Treat caches and prefetch state as derived accelerators only; use explicit cache families, invalidation reasons, warm sources, and bypass behavior; keep prefetch bounded and policy-preserving.
acceptance_criteria: Cache families, invalidation rules, and bypass conditions are explicit and inspectable; observability exposes hit, miss, bypass, invalidation, candidate-count, and generation-status signals; cache paths preserve policy boundaries.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 20 and cache observability expectations; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md observability hook contract.

title: Implement cache key families, admission rules, and hot-path lookup order
description: Break caching into explicit contracts rather than implementation folklore. This bead should define cache families, admission or bypass rules, and the hot-path lookup order that later observability and prefetch work build on.
design: Describe cache key construction and admission policy in a way that keeps lookup work bounded and makes bypass cases first-class.
acceptance_criteria: Done means cache families and lookup order are explicit, unit tests cover admission decisions, and logs show why a representative request was admitted, bypassed, or missed.
notes: Refs: docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md and docs/PLAN.md section 20. Cache work must keep event taxonomy, trace stages, candidate counts, and regression metrics explicit.

title: Implement bounded prefetch triggers, warm sources, and bypass reasons
description: Attach bounded prefetch behavior to the cache model without letting speculative work sprawl. This bead should define when prefetch may run, what warm source labels mean, and what explicit bypass reasons exist.
design: Keep speculative work subordinate to measurable budgets and expose warm-source provenance instead of hiding it in background jobs.
acceptance_criteria: Done means prefetch triggers are named, warm-source and bypass enums are explicit, and tests or scripts prove speculative work can be traced and bounded.
notes: Refs: docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md and docs/PLAN.md section 20. Cache work must keep event taxonomy, trace stages, candidate counts, and regression metrics explicit.

title: Implement cache trace stages, metrics fields, and per-family observability
description: Define the exact traces and metrics that later operators will use to debug caching behavior. This bead should preserve candidate counts and per-family outcomes so cache wins and regressions are both obvious.
design: Use the existing cache observability framing to name trace stages, per-request counters, per-family metrics, and the expected structure of explain or inspect outputs.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the metrics and trace fields, requires integration tests or scripts with detailed logs, and proves that multi-family requests preserve per-family outcome detail.
notes: Refs: docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md and docs/PLAN.md section 20. Cache work must keep event taxonomy, trace stages, candidate counts, and regression metrics explicit.

title: Implement cache invalidation, repair hooks, and regression tests
description: Close the cache loop by defining how cached state is invalidated or repaired when truth changes. This bead should link cache maintenance to the broader rebuild and regression story before operators are forced to guess.
design: Treat invalidation and repair as explicit contracts with named triggers, safe degraded behavior, and regression checks.
acceptance_criteria: Done means invalidation triggers are explicit, repair hooks are named for future ops work, and regression tests show stale cache state is corrected without violating request-path budgets.
notes: Refs: docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md and docs/PLAN.md section 20. Cache work must keep event taxonomy, trace stages, candidate counts, and regression metrics explicit.

title: Batch 2 feature follow-ons
description: Umbrella workstream for Feature Batch 2 in PLAN section 47. These are valuable later-stage follow-ons that must not reopen the phase gates or block the core execution spine.
design: Treat Batch 2 as deferred value rather than hidden prerequisites; attach each feature to the earliest sensible core contract, but keep dependencies one-way from follow-on to core.
acceptance_criteria: Child beads cover Features 11-20 with explicit links back to the core spine; no dependency from core beads points back into Batch 2.
notes: Canonical refs: PLAN section 47, especially 47.11 and 47.12.

title: Implement causal chain tracking (F11)
description: Implement typed causal links that explain why a memory or belief exists and how derived knowledge traces back to root evidence. This strengthens introspection without turning derived chains into a second source of truth.
design: Track typed parent-child causal links for reconsolidated, extracted, derived, or inferred memories; allow causal traces and invalidation penalties to propagate in a bounded, explainable way.
acceptance_criteria: Causal links are durable, queryable, and bounded enough for why-style traces and invalidation reports; invalidation penalties are explainable and do not silently delete the chain; this feature remains additive and non-blocking.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.1. Section 47 marks this as later-stage follow-on scope.

title: Implement causal-link schema and evidence attribution rules
description: Break causal chain tracking into the first durable layer: how causal links are represented and what evidence is allowed to support them. This bead should keep causal claims explicit and source-backed.
design: Model causal links as inspectable structures with provenance and confidence rather than hidden inference.
acceptance_criteria: Done means causal-link storage and attribution rules are explicit, tests cover representative chains, and logs show how sample evidence established a causal link.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement causal-chain recall and explain surfaces
