description: Turn causal links into retrieval value without making them mysterious. This bead should define how causal chains are recalled, traversed, and explained to callers.
design: Reuse graph and explain contracts so causal-chain retrieval remains bounded and debuggable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means recall examples show causal traversal, explain outputs reference chain evidence, and tests prove traversal remains within declared caps.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement query intent classification and auto-routing (F20)
description: Implement lightweight query intent classification and auto-routing so natural-language asks can map onto the canonical recall configuration. This remains a thin wrapper over existing capabilities instead of inventing an unbounded question-answer system.
design: Use fast pattern-based classification to choose among already-supported retrieval modes and output formatters; keep explain-intent and override-intent paths explicit so operators can inspect or bypass automatic routing.
acceptance_criteria: Intent routing produces a stable classification, confidence score, and mapped retrieval configuration; override and explain surfaces make automatic behavior auditable; the feature reuses canonical retrieval results and remains non-blocking.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.10. Later-stage follow-on scope; should sit on top of already-implemented retrieval families.

title: Implement intent taxonomy and routing-classification inputs
description: Break query intent routing into the first explicit layer: what intents exist and what signals classify them. This bead should define the routing taxonomy before any classifier or rule engine is implemented.
design: Treat routing intent as a structured, inspectable category rather than hidden prompt magic.
acceptance_criteria: Done means intent classes and classification inputs are explicit, fixtures cover representative queries, and logs show how a sample query was classified.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement auto-routing decisions, overrides, and explain outputs
description: Turn classified intent into actual planner choice while keeping the decision inspectable. This bead should define routing outputs, manual overrides, and the structured explanation for the chosen path.
design: Reuse retrieval planner and explain contracts so auto-routing remains bounded and debuggable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means routing choices, override behavior, and explain outputs are explicit, and examples show how two different intents choose different retrieval paths.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement memory snapshots and time-travel recall (F12)
description: Implement named snapshots and as-of recall so the system can answer what it knew at a prior point in time. This reuses the canonical retrieval pipeline while changing time anchors and result framing explicitly.
design: Store snapshots as lightweight metadata anchored to ticks rather than data copies; recompute effective strength and visibility as-of a snapshot while preserving the shared retrieval result contract.
acceptance_criteria: Named snapshots can be created, listed, deleted, and used as retrieval anchors without data-copy side effects; as-of recall explains the applied snapshot or tick context rather than pretending results are current-state answers.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.2 and section 10.10 cross-reference. Later-stage follow-on scope per section 47.

title: Implement named snapshot capture, storage, and retention rules
description: Break snapshots into the first durable layer: how named historical captures are created and retained. This bead should define what a snapshot contains, when it can be taken, and how long it lives.
design: Model snapshots as explicit historical artifacts with retention and governance semantics rather than as hidden backups.
acceptance_criteria: Done means snapshot capture rules are explicit, storage shape is named, and tests prove a representative snapshot can be created and retained correctly.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement time-travel retrieval planner and historical explain outputs
description: Turn snapshots into a usable retrieval mode by defining historical lookup behavior. This bead should explain how time-travel retrieval selects a snapshot or historical window and how results explain their historical context.
design: Keep historical recall bounded and explicit about what snapshot or time boundary it used.
acceptance_criteria: Done means time-travel retrieval branches are explicit, explain outputs include historical context, and examples show how present and historical recalls differ.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement attention heatmap and adaptive pre-warming (F13)
description: Implement retrieval event logging and hot-path aggregation so the system can expose attention heatmaps and use them for bounded prewarming. This improves observability and startup behavior without making prewarm state authoritative.
design: Log recall events, query patterns, latency, and retrieved IDs in a bounded retention structure; rebuild hot-path summaries from the log; use the derived heatmap only for operator views and bounded Tier1 prewarm decisions.
acceptance_criteria: Recall logging is bounded, namespace-aware, and useful for operator views and derived hot-path caches; prewarm behavior remains bounded, cancellable, and subordinate to current policy and correctness semantics; the feature does not become a core prerequisite.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.3. Later-stage follow-on scope; depends on core cache and retrieval observability.

title: Implement attention-signal aggregation and hotspot detection
description: Break the attention heatmap into the first analysis layer: how attention signals are aggregated into hotspots. This bead should define what inputs contribute and how they are rolled up over time or namespace.
design: Treat attention as an inspectable aggregate derived from real usage and retrieval behavior, not an opaque importance score.
acceptance_criteria: Done means attention inputs and hotspot rules are explicit, tests cover representative aggregates, and logs show how a sample hotspot was identified.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement adaptive pre-warming and attention heatmap views
description: Turn attention signals into user-visible heatmaps and bounded pre-warming behavior. This bead should define how hotspots drive pre-warming and how operators inspect the resulting heatmap.
design: Keep adaptive pre-warming bounded and explainable so it remains subordinate to the cache contract.
acceptance_criteria: Done means pre-warming triggers and heatmap views are explicit, examples show hotspot-driven warming, and tests prove the feature does not violate cache budgets.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement semantic diff across ticks and snapshots (F14)
description: Implement semantic diff so operators or agents can see how the memory system changed between two points in time. This packages change categories clearly without fabricating certainty or consensus.
design: Compute diffs over ticks or named snapshots using existing durable and derived metadata; summarize new, strengthened, weakened, archived, conflicting, and derived-state changes in explicit categories.
acceptance_criteria: Semantic diff can compare tick ranges and named snapshots with stable category semantics; diff output does not hide unresolved conflicts or conflate preference with truth; this feature remains additive and non-blocking.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.4. Later-stage follow-on scope; builds on snapshots and existing mutation history.

title: Implement semantic comparison inputs and snapshot diff model
description: Break semantic diff into the first comparison layer: how two memory states or snapshots are normalized for comparison. This bead should define the comparison inputs and diff representation.
design: Treat semantic diffs as structured, inspectable artifacts rather than UI-only text.
acceptance_criteria: Done means comparison inputs and diff schema are explicit, test fixtures cover representative changes, and logs show how a sample pair of states produced a diff.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement semantic diff inspect and explanation surfaces
description: Expose semantic diffs as an inspectable surface once the comparison model exists. This bead should define how callers explore changed facts, relations, or summaries between two states.
design: Reuse explain and provenance contracts so diffs remain source-backed and debuggable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means diff outputs are explicit, examples cover added, removed, and changed meaning, and logs show how a representative diff was explained.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement fork and merge brain states (F15)
description: Implement namespace-level fork and merge flows so specialized branches of memory can diverge and later reconcile. This should behave like governed branch-and-merge rather than unrestricted state cloning.
design: Fork by reference where safe so the feature reuses governed visibility semantics instead of copying the world; route merge conflicts through contradiction machinery instead of overwriting target knowledge.
acceptance_criteria: Fork metadata preserves parent namespace, inherited visibility, and status cleanly; merge reports name merged items and conflict counts; policy and visibility rules are enforced before fork or merge surfaces touch expensive work.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.5. Later-stage follow-on scope; explicitly depends on sharing and contradiction surfaces.

title: Implement fork capture, branch metadata, and isolation semantics
description: Break fork and merge into the first safe layer: how a forked state is created and isolated. This bead should define branch metadata, namespace or visibility semantics, and what it means for a branch to diverge.
design: Model forks as explicit branch artifacts with isolation guarantees rather than hidden copies.
acceptance_criteria: Done means fork semantics are explicit, branch metadata is named, and tests prove a forked state is isolated from the source until merge decisions occur.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement merge resolution, conflict reporting, and audit trails
description: Turn branching into a usable workflow by defining how merges happen and how conflicts are reported. This bead should keep merges explicit, contradiction-aware, and auditable.
design: Reuse contradiction and audit contracts so merge behavior never silently overwrites divergent state.
acceptance_criteria: Done means merge rules and conflict outputs are explicit, audit trails are required, and examples show a representative merge conflict and resolution path.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement predictive pre-recall (F16)
description: Implement learned recall-sequence tracking and bounded speculative prewarm so the system can prepare likely next queries. This should feel like an accelerator layered on top of the heatmap and cache contracts, not a new correctness path.
design: Learn simple query transitions from observed recall sequences within bounded retention and confidence thresholds; trigger speculative prewarm only under bounded cache and pressure conditions.
acceptance_criteria: Transition learning and prewarm behavior are bounded, inspectable, and optional; prediction results expose confidence and hit-rate metrics; speculative work respects current pressure and does not degrade foreground correctness.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.6. Later-stage follow-on scope; depends on heatmap and cache infrastructure.

title: Implement predictive pre-recall signals and bounded trigger rules
description: Break predictive pre-recall into the first bounded heuristic layer. This bead should define what signals may trigger predictive recall preparation and what hard limits keep it from becoming unbounded background work.
design: Treat predictive behavior as optional and bounded, with explicit triggers and skip reasons.
acceptance_criteria: Done means predictive triggers and caps are explicit, tests prove bounded behavior, and logs show why a representative predictive action fired or was skipped.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement predictive-pre-recall observability and fallback behavior
description: Attach inspection and fallback semantics to predictive behavior so it does not become invisible background magic. This bead should define how predictive actions are surfaced and what happens when they are bypassed or wrong.
design: Reuse cache and explain contracts so predictive work remains observable and safe to disable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means predictive observability fields and fallback rules are explicit, and examples show both a successful and a bypassed predictive case.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement memory schema compression (F17)
description: Implement schema compression that synthesizes cross-episode situation patterns into durable schema memories. This should reduce noise and storage pressure while preserving source lineage and avoiding premature abstraction.
design: Detect compressible episodic clusters with explicit size and coherence thresholds; create schema memories as derived artifacts that point back to source episodes and preserve reversibility where practical.
acceptance_criteria: Compression reports expose created schemas, source counts, and storage or utility effects; source episodes retain enough lineage and reversibility to audit or undo compression; this feature remains optional and non-blocking.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.7 and critical M1 schema additions in 47.12. Later-stage follow-on scope.

title: Implement compression candidate selection and safety constraints
description: Break schema compression into the first conservative layer: deciding what is even eligible for compression. This bead should define candidate selection and the safety rules that prevent loss of authoritative evidence.
design: Treat compression as reversible or at least safety-constrained derived-state optimization, not casual data loss.
acceptance_criteria: Done means compression eligibility and safety constraints are explicit, tests cover protected cases, and logs show why a representative artifact was or was not eligible.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement compressed representation storage and reconstructability checks
description: Turn eligible compression into a concrete representation and validation story. This bead should define how compressed artifacts are stored and how reconstructability is checked.
design: Require reconstructability evidence so compression remains subordinate to durable truth.
acceptance_criteria: Done means compressed representation rules are explicit, reconstructability tests are named, and logs show a sample artifact compressing and being verified successfully.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement emotional trajectory tracking (F18)
description: Implement emotional trajectory tracking so the system can observe mood timelines and optionally use mood-congruent retrieval. This stays inspectable and opt-in rather than silently biasing all recall.
design: Capture encoding-time valence and arousal snapshots and roll them into bounded timeline aggregates; treat mood-congruent retrieval as an explicit optional influence with visible scoring or flag output.
acceptance_criteria: Mood timeline rows and encoding-time emotional fields are queryable and namespace-aware; mood-congruent recall is opt-in, explainable, and bounded in influence; this feature remains later-stage and non-blocking.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.8 and critical M1 schema additions in 47.12. Later-stage follow-on scope.

title: Implement affect-signal capture and trajectory storage model
description: Break emotional trajectory tracking into the first durable layer: how affect signals are captured and represented over time. This bead should define what signals are recorded and how the trajectory is stored.
design: Treat affect tracking as explicit, inspectable metadata rather than hidden inference.
acceptance_criteria: Done means affect-signal storage is explicit, tests cover representative trajectories, and logs show how a sample trajectory accumulated over time.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement emotional-trajectory recall and dashboard integration
description: Turn stored affect trajectories into user-facing value by defining recall and dashboard behavior. This bead should explain how trajectories are queried and where they appear in health or history views.
design: Reuse dashboard and explain surfaces so affect history remains inspectable and policy-aware.
acceptance_criteria: Done means recall and dashboard surfaces are explicit, examples show a representative trajectory query, and tests prove policy-aware presentation.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement write-ahead memory audit log (F19)
description: Implement the immutable mutation log that records important memory operations and their before-or-after state changes. This should make forensics and operator trust easier without becoming a hidden performance cliff.
design: Append audit entries for encode, recall-driven strengthening, archive, reconsolidate, interference, consolidation, compression, and other meaningful mutations; keep retention bounded and purge behavior explicit.
acceptance_criteria: Audit rows capture operation type, tick, before or after strength or confidence where relevant, trigger source, and namespace context; audit surfaces can return per-memory history and filtered range views; bounded-overhead evidence exists.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 47.9 and critical M1 schema additions in 47.12. The section explicitly marks this as additive and micro-overhead.

title: Implement append-only audit-log storage and event taxonomy
description: Break the write-ahead memory audit log into the first durable layer: the append-only event stream and taxonomy. This bead should define which events are recorded and how they are categorized.
design: Treat the audit log as append-only authoritative history for sensitive operations, not as best-effort debug output.
acceptance_criteria: Done means event taxonomy and append-only storage are explicit, tests prove events append in order, and logs show representative encode, policy, and maintenance events entering the log.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Implement audit-log query, filter, and export surfaces
description: Turn the raw audit stream into a usable operator surface. This bead should define filtering, querying, and export behavior for audit history across time, namespace, or event family.
design: Keep audit query behavior bounded and structured so exports remain predictable and policy-aware.
acceptance_criteria: Done means filter and export rules are explicit, examples cover representative audit queries, and scripts show how operators inspect a slice of audit history.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 46 and 47 plus the relevant subsystem docs. Each feature bead must stay self-contained, bounded, and explicit about tests, explainability, and policy interactions.

title: Maintenance, quality, and operations
description: Umbrella workstream for consolidation, forgetting, repair, deterministic tests, benchmarks, operational tooling, and scale-out gates. It makes the system safe to trust after the core spine exists.
design: Treat derived state as rebuildable and foreground latency as hard; require deterministic semantic tests plus benchmark artifacts before promotion; defer scale-out until single-node repair and ops are proven.
acceptance_criteria: Child beads cover maintenance, validation, runbooks, and measured-demand scale-out evaluation; dependencies prevent ops maturity claims before repair and evidence are in place.
notes: Canonical refs: PLAN sections 21-31, 38-40, 44; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md evidence matrix and PR rejection rules.

title: Implement consolidation pipeline with lineage-preserving derivations
description: Build the consolidation pipeline that turns raw evidence into summaries, facts, relations, and other derived utility without breaking provenance. This is the backbone for later skill extraction, compression, and forgetting work.
design: Form episodes, summaries, extracted facts, and reinforced relations only when source lineage remains queryable and auditable; keep consolidation background-bounded and repairable rather than mixing it into foreground retrieval.
acceptance_criteria: Derived artifacts preserve source references, timestamps, namespace context, and contradiction semantics; background execution stays bounded; failure handling preserves durable truth and records partial derivations explicitly.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 21, phase-3 gate in 40.4, final thesis in 45, and contributor repairability rules.

title: Implement episode formation and source-set grouping rules
description: Break consolidation into the first bounded grouping step: how raw items are clustered into episodes or source sets before higher-order derivations appear. This bead should make grouping rules explicit and replayable.
design: Define the temporal, entity, or source-based grouping heuristics and the lineage fields that record why a set of inputs was grouped together.
acceptance_criteria: Done means grouping rules are explicit, deterministic fixtures are named, and logs show which source items formed a sample episode and why.
notes: Refs: docs/CONSOLIDATION.md and docs/PLAN.md section 21. Derived artifacts must preserve lineage, namespace context, and explicit partial-failure reporting.

title: Implement summary and gist derivations with lineage preservation
description: Turn grouped evidence into bounded summary-like artifacts without losing provenance. This bead should explain how summaries are formed, how they point back to source evidence, and how partial failures are represented.
design: Keep summary derivation background-bounded, lineage-preserving, and explicit about whether the derived artifact is complete or partial.
acceptance_criteria: Done means summary artifacts retain source references and namespace context, partial derivations are explicit, and integration tests prove the lineage survives consolidation.
notes: Refs: docs/CONSOLIDATION.md and docs/PLAN.md section 21. Derived artifacts must preserve lineage, namespace context, and explicit partial-failure reporting.

title: Implement fact, relation, and reinforcement derivations
description: Extend consolidation beyond summaries into structured facts and reinforced relations. This bead should explain what structured derivations are allowed, how they cite sources, and how contradiction-aware reinforcement works.
design: Model facts and relations as derived artifacts with explicit provenance, confidence, and contradiction sensitivity rather than opaque knowledge blobs.
acceptance_criteria: Done means derived facts and relations keep source lineage, contradiction-aware tests are named, and explain outputs can show how a representative relation was formed.
notes: Refs: docs/CONSOLIDATION.md and docs/PLAN.md section 21. Derived artifacts must preserve lineage, namespace context, and explicit partial-failure reporting.

title: Implement consolidation scheduler, queue bounds, and partial-failure handling
description: Attach operational boundaries to consolidation before it becomes a hidden background sink. This bead should define queue bounds, retry behavior, cancellation, and the artifacts emitted when a run is partial or interrupted.
design: Keep consolidation work cancellable and bounded, with explicit queue and retry policies tied to observable operator metrics.
acceptance_criteria: Done means queue and retry bounds are explicit, failure-handling tests or scripts are named, and telemetry captures job duration, queue depth, affected-item counts, and partial-run status.
notes: Refs: docs/CONSOLIDATION.md and docs/PLAN.md section 21. Derived artifacts must preserve lineage, namespace context, and explicit partial-failure reporting.

title: Implement forgetting, demotion, archive, and restore semantics
description: Implement the bounded forgetting and demotion pipeline that reduces noise without silently deleting truth. It must keep utility forgetting, archive or restore, and policy deletion as separate concepts.
design: Support suppress, decay, demote, summarize, archive, restore, redact, soft-delete, and hard-delete style flows with explicit policy boundaries; protect last-authoritative evidence and legal holds.
acceptance_criteria: Forgetting flows are auditable and distinguish utility reduction from policy-driven deletion; governance analysis covers retention and last-authoritative-evidence protections; archive and restore semantics are explicit enough for round-trip tests.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 22 and governance matrix in 24; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md forgetting or deletion evidence row.

title: Implement forgetting eligibility scoring and reversible demotion rules
description: Break forgetting into the first reversible decision layer: how items become eligible for demotion or archive without being silently destroyed. This bead should make the scoring and eligibility rules explicit.
design: Define demotion eligibility using lifecycle, recency, access, contradiction, and policy factors while preserving reversibility by default.
acceptance_criteria: Done means forgetting eligibility rules are explicit, deterministic tests are named, and logs show why a sample item was kept, demoted, or marked ineligible.
notes: Refs: docs/FORGETTING.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md section 22. Archive, restore, and deletion behavior must stay reversible by default unless policy explicitly says otherwise.

title: Implement archive storage, restore flow, and tombstone handling
description: Turn reversibility into a concrete storage and workflow contract. This bead should describe how archived items are represented, how restore works, and how tombstones or loss indicators are surfaced.
design: Keep archive and restore behavior explicit, lineage-preserving, and policy-aware rather than hidden behind storage shortcuts.
acceptance_criteria: Done means archive records, restore paths, and tombstone behavior are explicit, with integration tests or scripts proving restore preserves the right metadata and explain signals.
notes: Refs: docs/FORGETTING.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md section 22. Archive, restore, and deletion behavior must stay reversible by default unless policy explicitly says otherwise.

title: Implement policy-gated irreversible deletion and legal-hold denial paths
description: Isolate the truly irreversible path so it cannot hide inside general forgetting logic. This bead should define the explicit authority, denials, and audit trails required before hard deletion may happen.
design: Treat irreversible deletion as a governance-sensitive path with dedicated legal-hold checks, authority checks, and denial artifacts.
acceptance_criteria: Done means hard-delete preconditions and denials are explicit, dedicated policy tests are required, and audit records show who requested deletion and why it was allowed or denied.
notes: Refs: docs/FORGETTING.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md section 22. Archive, restore, and deletion behavior must stay reversible by default unless policy explicitly says otherwise.

title: Implement forgetting explain, audit, and operator review surfaces
description: Expose forgetting outcomes so operators can inspect what changed instead of trusting a background job blindly. This bead should define the explain or audit outputs for demotion, archive, restore, and deletion decisions.
design: Reuse the governance and observability contracts so forgetting work remains inspectable and reviewable after the fact.
acceptance_criteria: Done means forgetting actions produce named audit and explain artifacts, logging-heavy scripts are required, and later operators can review why a representative item changed state.
notes: Refs: docs/FORGETTING.md, docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md, and docs/PLAN.md section 22. Archive, restore, and deletion behavior must stay reversible by default unless policy explicitly says otherwise.

title: Implement lease policies, freshness states, and bounded lease scanning
description: Promote Section 10.3 Memory Leases into a first-class freshness and staleness-management bead. This bead should define lease policies and freshness states for memory-like objects so stale or action-critical knowledge can degrade explicitly instead of remaining silently trusted forever.
design: Add lease_policy, freshness_state, and lease-expiry semantics to the relevant durable objects; define policy classes such as volatile, normal, durable, and pinned; and implement a bounded background lease scanner that only performs state transitions or lightweight checks rather than expensive recalculation on the request path.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead defines lease policy classes and freshness states explicitly; requires deterministic tests for transitions among fresh, stale, and pinned states; names the bounded scanner behavior; and requires logs showing how a representative stale action-critical item is downgraded, recheck-flagged, or withheld from high-confidence output.
notes: Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 10.3 plus sections 14, 22, 24, and 38. This fills the current bead-graph gap where freshness and forgetting existed but lease policy was not explicit.

title: Implement LTP/LTD strength updates and lazy Ebbinghaus decay
description: Implement the dynamic strength engine that applies recall-side LTP, non-use LTD, and lazy Ebbinghaus-style decay without relying on wall-clock sleeps. This bead owns strength, stability, last-access, access-count, bypass-decay, and archive-threshold transitions so memory strength is a real state machine instead of a static score field.
design: Separate dynamic strength mutation from ranking-only formulas. On successful recall, apply bounded LTP and stability increments; on interaction-tick or pressure-triggered decay passes, apply LTD/Ebbinghaus weakening from deterministic tick sources; respect emotional bypass-decay rules; and archive rather than hard-delete when strength drops below threshold. Keep the mutation surfaces inspectable and replayable.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core code implements LTP/LTD and lazy-decay state transitions, deterministic fixtures prove monotonic LTP, bounded LTD decay, bypass-decay behavior, archive-not-delete thresholds, and no wall-clock dependence, and logging-heavy integration artifacts show recall strengthening, pressure-triggered decay, and archive decisions with tick-aware traces.
notes: Why: PLAN sections 2.5, 4.3, and 5.2 make LTP/LTD the core learning mechanism, yet the backlog did not contain a dedicated code bead for it. This bead restores a missing central mechanism that downstream forgetting, confidence, and reconsolidation logic all assume exists. Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 2.5, 4.3, and 5.2.

title: Implement proactive and retroactive interference handling
description: Implement proactive and retroactive interference handling for near-duplicate but non-identical memories. This bead owns similarity-band detection, penalty rules, event logging, and the separation between duplicates, contradictions, and interference so overlapping memories can affect one another without collapsing into silent overwrite.
design: Use bounded similarity search over the existing retrieval substrate to detect candidates in the interference band while excluding exact duplicates and explicit contradiction cases. Apply retroactive penalties when new memories weaken similar older ones, record proactive difficulty when old memories can confuse newer recall, and emit inspectable interference events for later confidence and audit surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Done means code implements bounded interference detection and penalty application, unit or integration tests cover duplicate cutoff versus interference band, proactive versus retroactive cases, and no hidden full scans, and structured artifacts show which memories interfered, why the path was classified as interference instead of duplicate or contradiction, and what penalties were applied.
notes: Why: PLAN sections 4.7 and 4.9 make interference a distinct biological mechanism with real product consequences, but the backlog previously mentioned it only indirectly. This bead adds the missing implementation unit that confidence, forgetting, and audit work can build upon. Refs: docs/PLAN.md sections 4.7, 4.9, and 5.2.

title: Implement compaction, repair, and rebuild machinery
description: Implement compaction, repair, and rebuild paths that can recover derived state from durable truth under failure. This owns the maintenance machinery that keeps the system safe after corruption, drift, or partial migrations.
design: Treat indexes, graph state, caches, and summary-like artifacts as rebuildable from durable truth with explicit repair reports; keep compaction and repair out of the request path and instrument their foreground impact.
acceptance_criteria: Repair and rebuild procedures name their durable sources of truth and recovery outputs; failure-injection tests cover partial corruption and interrupted repair; telemetry exposes duration, affected-item counts, queue depth, error counts, and rollback state.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 23, 25, 26, 40.3-40.5, 44 step 10; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md repair and background-ops evidence rows.

title: Implement index rebuild entrypoints and verification artifacts
description: Break repair into the first rebuildable subsystem: indexes. This bead should define how indexes are rebuilt from durable truth and how operators verify the rebuild was correct.
design: Treat index rebuild as a deterministic maintenance routine with named entrypoints, source-of-truth references, and verification outputs.
acceptance_criteria: Done means rebuild entrypoints and verification artifacts are explicit, failure-injection tests are named, and logs show what was rebuilt and whether verification passed.
notes: Refs: docs/COMPACTION_AND_REPAIR.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 23, 25, and 26. Repair work must rebuild from durable truth and surface clear operator telemetry.

title: Implement graph and cache rebuild flows from durable truth
description: Extend repair from indexes into the other major derived-state surfaces. This bead should explain how graph and cache state are reconstructed or invalidated from durable truth after drift or corruption.
design: Keep graph and cache rebuild logic separate but aligned around the same durable-truth-first repair principles.
acceptance_criteria: Done means graph and cache repair entrypoints are explicit, recovery artifacts are named, and failure tests prove stale or corrupted derived state can be repaired safely.
notes: Refs: docs/COMPACTION_AND_REPAIR.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 23, 25, and 26. Repair work must rebuild from durable truth and surface clear operator telemetry.

title: Implement compaction planning, batching, and cancellation safety
description: Turn compaction into a bounded maintenance task instead of an opaque bulk job. This bead should define compaction unit selection, batch sizing, cancellation safety, and the relationship between compaction and durable truth.
design: Keep compaction background-only, cancellable, and explicit about what it may rewrite versus what remains authoritative.
acceptance_criteria: Done means compaction batches and cancellation behavior are explicit, tests or scripts cover interruption safety, and logs show batch sizes, duration, and any skipped work.
notes: Refs: docs/COMPACTION_AND_REPAIR.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 23, 25, and 26. Repair work must rebuild from durable truth and surface clear operator telemetry.

title: Implement repair reports, degraded-mode transitions, and rollback triggers
description: Close the repair loop with operator-facing outputs and safe fallback behavior. This bead should define the reports emitted by repair or compaction runs and the conditions that trigger degraded mode or rollback.
design: Connect repair execution to explicit status reporting and containment behavior rather than leaving operators to infer system health from raw logs.
acceptance_criteria: Done means repair reports and degraded-mode triggers are explicit, rollback conditions are named, and e2e scripts show how a simulated failure transitions the system safely.
notes: Refs: docs/COMPACTION_AND_REPAIR.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 23, 25, and 26. Repair work must rebuild from durable truth and surface clear operator telemetry.

title: Implement deterministic tests and failure-injection coverage
description: Build the deterministic semantic test suites that prove lifecycle, policy, ranking, forgetting, and repair rules without relying on ambient time or flaky sleeps. It also owns failure-injection coverage for the maintenance paths.
design: Use logical ticks, injected clocks, and explicit fixtures for semantic behavior that depends on time or decay; cover formula correctness, round-trip retrieval, namespace isolation, denial behavior, and repairability in dedicated suites.
acceptance_criteria: Time-sensitive semantic tests avoid wall-clock dependence except in true elapsed-time benchmark contexts; isolation, denial, contradiction, repair, and rebuild scenarios have dedicated coverage and failure-injection artifacts.
notes: Refs: PLAN section 28; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md testing requirements and determinism review rules.

title: Implement deterministic fixtures for lifecycle, decay, and timeout semantics
description: Break the deterministic test surface into the highest-risk semantic areas first. This bead should define the reusable fixtures for lifecycle transitions, decay rules, recency behavior, and timeout or retry semantics.
design: Provide logical clocks, ticks, and fixture builders that downstream tests can reuse instead of inventing wall-clock sleeps.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the deterministic fixtures are named, reusable, and demonstrated by tests covering representative lifecycle and timing-sensitive behaviors.
notes: Refs: docs/TEST_STRATEGY.md and docs/PLAN.md section 28. Time-sensitive correctness tests must be deterministic; e2e scripts should emit rich logs, not just pass or fail bits.

title: Implement encode-to-recall round-trip and policy integration tests
description: Define the core high-value integration tests that prove the system still works end to end. This bead should cover encode, recall, contradiction, and policy-sensitive denial or redaction behavior across the main retrieval spine.
design: Treat round-trip integration tests as contract proof rather than happy-path smoke tests by including policy and contradiction scenarios.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the integration scenarios, requires detailed logs, and proves the main retrieval spine and denial paths work without relying on hidden assumptions.
notes: Refs: docs/TEST_STRATEGY.md and docs/PLAN.md section 28. Time-sensitive correctness tests must be deterministic; e2e scripts should emit rich logs, not just pass or fail bits.

title: Implement failure-injection scenarios for repair, consolidation, and archive flows
description: Exercise the system under partial failure instead of assuming maintenance paths always succeed. This bead should define targeted failure-injection scenarios across repair, consolidation, forgetting, and restore behavior.
design: Use deterministic hooks or fixtures so partial failures are reproducible and their operator-visible artifacts can be asserted.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names concrete injected failures, expected degraded outputs, and the logs or metrics that confirm containment worked.
notes: Refs: docs/TEST_STRATEGY.md and docs/PLAN.md section 28. Time-sensitive correctness tests must be deterministic; e2e scripts should emit rich logs, not just pass or fail bits.

title: Implement logging-heavy end-to-end scripts for major workflows
description: Create the scriptable, human-readable proof layer that flywheel-beads expects on major work. This bead should cover logging-heavy e2e scripts for retrieval, contradiction, policy denial, consolidation, forgetting, and repair flows.
design: Treat these scripts as canonical demonstrations that future contributors can rerun when validating or polishing related work.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the e2e scripts, the logs they must emit, and the acceptance artifacts each script should leave behind.
notes: Refs: docs/TEST_STRATEGY.md and docs/PLAN.md section 28. Time-sensitive correctness tests must be deterministic; e2e scripts should emit rich logs, not just pass or fail bits.

title: Implement benchmark harness and regression artifacts
description: Build the representative benchmark harnesses and artifact format that the core, cache, maintenance, and operations beads depend on. This turns performance claims into rerunnable evidence instead of intuition.
design: Cover Tier1, Tier2, Tier3, ranking, cache, repair, and other touched hot or background paths with representative corpora and declared metadata; treat regressions as contract failures until explained.
acceptance_criteria: Benchmark harnesses exist for paths named in the canonical performance budget and speed checklist; artifacts record dataset cardinality, machine profile, build mode, warm or cold condition, harness entrypoint, and location.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 27, 38, 39, 40; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md benchmark restrictions and observability hook contract.

title: Implement benchmark harness entrypoints for hot-path retrieval and encode
description: Break benchmark work into the first reusable harness layer. This bead should define how encode and retrieval hot paths are benchmarked, parameterized, and rerun reproducibly.
design: Build explicit benchmark entrypoints with stable arguments, dataset labels, and artifact locations so later optimizations can compare like with like.
acceptance_criteria: Done means harness entrypoints are named, required metadata fields are explicit, and sample benchmark artifacts demonstrate rerunnable encode and retrieval measurements.
notes: Refs: docs/BENCHMARKS.md, docs/PERFORMANCE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/HOT_PATH_OPTIMIZATION.md. Benchmarks need dataset, machine, build, warm/cold, harness, and artifact metadata.

title: Implement representative datasets and warm-versus-cold benchmark modes
description: Attach representative data and warm-state control to the harness so benchmark claims stay interpretable. This bead should describe dataset families, cardinality labels, and how warm versus cold runs are expressed.
design: Keep dataset and warm-state declarations explicit so later results are not overclaimed or mislabeled as production facts.
acceptance_criteria: Done means benchmark datasets and warm or cold modes are named, representativeness labels are explicit, and sample results include the required metadata.
notes: Refs: docs/BENCHMARKS.md, docs/PERFORMANCE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/HOT_PATH_OPTIMIZATION.md. Benchmarks need dataset, machine, build, warm/cold, harness, and artifact metadata.

title: Implement benchmark artifact publishing and regression comparison workflow
description: Turn benchmark execution into useful regression evidence rather than one-off numbers. This bead should describe artifact formats, where results live, and how future runs compare against a prior baseline.
design: Treat benchmark outputs as auditable artifacts with metadata, summaries, and a clear comparison method.
acceptance_criteria: Done means artifact formats and comparison steps are explicit, regression thresholds or review expectations are named, and later contributors can rerun and compare a benchmark without guessing.
notes: Refs: docs/BENCHMARKS.md, docs/PERFORMANCE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/HOT_PATH_OPTIMIZATION.md. Benchmarks need dataset, machine, build, warm/cold, harness, and artifact metadata.

title: Implement doctor, degraded-mode, and runbook surfaces
description: Build the operator-facing doctor, degraded-mode, and runbook surfaces that make failure handling actionable. This turns maintenance and recovery semantics into something an operator or client can inspect and follow safely.
design: Expose availability posture, degraded reasons, repair queue depth, and backpressure state without mutating hidden system state; tie failure classes to concrete runbook steps, rollback conditions, and post-run validation checks.
acceptance_criteria: Doctor or operator surfaces exist for major failure classes and degraded serving states; runbooks name preconditions, commands, metrics to watch, rollback conditions, and post-run validation; health views remain read-only.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 25, 26, 31, 40.5, 46.10; docs/CONTRIBUTING.md operations and observability requirements.

title: Implement doctor checks for schema, index, graph, and cache health
description: Break operator tooling into the first concrete health surface: doctor checks. This bead should define the checks that inspect schema consistency, index state, graph health, and cache state without mutating truth.
design: Keep doctor behavior diagnostic and bounded, with clear pass, warn, and fail outputs that later operators can trust.
acceptance_criteria: Done means doctor checks and outputs are explicit, sample failures are named, and scripts or examples show how an operator would interpret the results.
notes: Refs: docs/OPERATIONS.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 25 and 26. Operator-facing status and degraded-mode behavior should be explicit and inspectable.

title: Implement degraded-mode status surfaces and operator guidance
description: Expose degraded-mode behavior directly instead of forcing operators to infer it from internals. This bead should define the status surfaces and operator guidance presented when a subsystem is partially unavailable or rebuilding.
design: Make degraded mode a first-class state with explicit user-visible consequences and recommended operator actions.
acceptance_criteria: Done means degraded-mode surfaces are explicit, examples show how callers are informed, and runbook-oriented logs or guidance are linked to the state.
notes: Refs: docs/OPERATIONS.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 25 and 26. Operator-facing status and degraded-mode behavior should be explicit and inspectable.

title: Implement runbook surfaces for repair, archive, and benchmark operations
description: Connect the system to the operator runbooks that will actually be used during incidents and validation. This bead should define the commands, outputs, and guidance surfaces that support repair, archive, restore, and benchmark workflows.
design: Treat runbook surfaces as explicit product affordances rather than implied documentation-only procedures.
acceptance_criteria: Done means operator entrypoints and expected outputs are named, logging-heavy examples are required, and later incident response work can cite this bead directly.
notes: Refs: docs/OPERATIONS.md, docs/FAILURE_PLAYBOOK.md, and docs/PLAN.md sections 25 and 26. Operator-facing status and degraded-mode behavior should be explicit and inspectable.

title: Evaluate sharding and distribution only under measured demand
description: Keep scale-out and distribution as explicitly gated follow-on work that only activates after the single-node system is benchmarked, repairable, and operationally understood. This defends the plan rule that scale-out is justified by workload pressure rather than aesthetics.
design: Treat sharding, replica, and movement strategies as non-default until workload evidence demands them; require governance, repair, and recovery semantics to survive cross-shard boundaries before promotion.
acceptance_criteria: The bead names workload signals that justify leaving the bounded single-node model; benchmark and recovery expectations are explicit for shard movement, repair, and recovery; no core bead depends on this issue.
notes: Refs: PLAN sections 29, 40.5, 44 step 13, and later-stage framing in docs/INDEX.md.

title: Define workload thresholds and evidence gates for sharding
description: Keep scale-out work grounded in evidence by defining the threshold conditions that justify it. This bead should say what workload signals or benchmark pressure must exist before distribution leaves the backlog and enters active implementation.
design: Express sharding as an evidence-gated follow-on with explicit workload, latency, and operational thresholds.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the gating thresholds are explicit and later scale-out work cannot claim urgency without benchmark or operational evidence that matches them.
notes: Refs: docs/SHARDING_AND_DISTRIBUTION.md and docs/PLAN.md section 29. Distribution remains evidence-gated later-stage work, not default architecture direction.

title: Define namespace, consistency, and repair implications for distribution
description: If the system ever scales out, governance and repair semantics will get harder. This bead should outline those implications before any distributed design is treated as default direction.
design: Analyze how namespace isolation, consistency, rebuildability, and repair workflows would change under distribution without forcing immediate implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Done means the bead names the main consistency and repair implications and preserves the single-node model as canonical until those implications are justified by real demand.
notes: Refs: docs/SHARDING_AND_DISTRIBUTION.md and docs/PLAN.md section 29. Distribution remains evidence-gated later-stage work, not default architecture direction.

title: Implement reconsolidation and labile-update lifecycle
description: Implement the reconsolidation lifecycle in which a successful recall can reopen a bounded labile window for later update and then restabilize safely. This feature owns the mutable-after-recall contract, pending-update submission rules, apply-versus-discard behavior, and the requirement that derived embedding, index, and cache state refresh after accepted reconsolidation updates.
design: Model reconsolidation as an explicit lifecycle overlay rather than a hidden side effect. Reopen a labile window only when age and strength rules allow it, keep pending updates explicit and bounded, apply accepted updates only while the window is valid, discard stale pending updates without mutating durable truth, and restabilize to the pre-reopen durable state after apply or expiry.
acceptance_criteria: Done means child beads cover labile-window reopening and pending updates plus apply/discard ticks and derived-state refresh, dependencies tie reconsolidation to strength, retrieval, and cache refresh foundations, and acceptance artifacts prove deterministic window math, no implicit pending updates, restabilization after expiry, and structured audit traces for applied versus discarded reconsolidation updates.
notes: Why: PLAN section 4.6 makes reconsolidation a canonical memory mechanism, but the current code-oriented backlog did not represent it as its own implementation stream. This feature restores that missing plan coverage and keeps future confidence and audit work from hand-waving over mutable-after-recall behavior. Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 4.6 and 5.2.

title: Implement labile-window reopening and pending-update submission rules
description: Implement the first half of reconsolidation: reopening the bounded labile window and accepting explicit pending updates while a recalled memory is temporarily mutable. This bead owns window eligibility, state capture, pending-update submission validation, and the rule that recall alone never creates a pending update.
design: Use deterministic age and strength inputs to compute window eligibility and duration. Persist the pre-reopen stable state, store explicit pending updates separately from the authoritative row, reject malformed or out-of-window submissions, and keep the whole flow namespace- and policy-aware. No hidden auto-merge or speculative mutation is allowed.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core code can reopen a valid labile window, reject ineligible recalls or malformed updates, and store pending updates without touching authoritative truth until apply time. Tests must cover young-versus-old memories, strong-versus-weak memories, no implicit pending update on recall, and structured traces naming the window, pre-reopen state, and submission outcome.
notes: Why: the window-opening and pending-update contract is distinct from later apply/discard behavior and needs its own bead so the lifecycle does not get collapsed into a single vague background job. Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 4.6.

title: Implement reconsolidation apply-discard tick and derived-state refresh
description: Implement the second half of reconsolidation: applying or discarding pending updates on the tick path, refreshing derived state, and restabilizing the memory afterward. This bead owns apply-time mutation, stale-update discard, cache/index/embed refresh, and the audit artifacts that prove what happened.
design: Drive apply/discard from deterministic tick state rather than sleeps. If the labile window is still valid and a pending update exists, mutate authoritative durable truth, refresh embeddings and derived indexes or caches through bounded hooks, award the reconsolidation bonus, and restabilize. If the window expired, discard the pending update, preserve the pre-reopen authoritative state, and emit an explicit stale-update artifact.
acceptance_criteria: Done means core code implements reconsolidation apply/discard ticks, bounded refresh of embeddings/indexes/caches after accepted updates, and audit-friendly artifacts for apply, discard, and restabilize outcomes. Tests must cover valid apply, expired discard, refresh-trigger emission, and no silent mutation when refresh of derived state fails or is deferred.
notes: Why: reconsolidation is only safe if apply-time mutation, discard, refresh, and restabilization are explicit and testable. This bead closes that loop instead of leaving derived-state repair implicit. Refs: docs/PLAN.md section 4.6 and 5.2.
